results.public_map_disclaimer: Research prototype: this map explores cognitive tendencies for self-understanding and open research. It maps everyday patterns on continuous dimensions and is not validated for individual clinical, legal, or placement decisions.
results.share_text: Summary from the Perceptual & Cognitive Mapping System — a research mapping tool for reflection.
results.footer_line1: Perceptual & Cognitive Mapping System · Research platform
results.footer_line2: Built for reflection and open research. For health concerns, consult qualified healthcare providers.
results.patterns_empty: No standout patterns were flagged from this session’s statistics.
results.notes_empty: No extra behavioural notes were added for this session.
results.sms_code_copied: Short code copied — coarse scores for SMS relay only.
results.research_body: Thank you for contributing to cognitive science research. Your anonymous session helps us study response patterns at a statistical level for instrument development and aggregate reporting.
results.research_consent_note: Your data has been stored anonymously and may be used for scientific research. No personal information was collected beyond what you explicitly consented to.
results.research_consent_local_only: This session is stored in your browser only. No research database upload ran because cloud storage is not configured for this deployment.
results.revision_notice: This is revision {n} of your assessment.
results.max_length_notice: Maximum assessment length reached. Results are based on all answers provided.
results.sufficient_confidence_banner: Sufficient confidence was reached on all routing dimensions before the usual number of core questions — your profile is based on the answers you gave.
results.share_url_too_long: This share link would exceed a safe URL length. Use “Download data” or a shorter results path, or share the JSON export instead.
results.title: Your cognitive profile
results.group_insights_hint: Opens aggregate, non-identifying guidance if your program has enabled cohort insights.
results.group_analysis_hint: Optional: combine 2–6 share payloads to see diversity, environment fit, and facilitation hints together (research / team contexts).
results.research_bundle_no_history: No answer history found in this browser — research ZIP needs stored responses from this device.
results.download_full_session_no_history: No answer history found in this browser — full-session export needs stored responses from this device.
results.print_footer: Perceptual & Cognitive Mapping System — descriptive research summary for self-understanding and research records.
results.print_popup_blocked: Allow pop-ups for this site to print, or use your browser print on the results page.

eight_constructs.heading: Eight construct scales (this session)
eight_constructs.intro: These values summarise your answers on the global behavioral bank. They are reported separately from the ten routing dimensions (F–V) used for the adaptive engine and landscape—both layers are kept for scientific transparency.
eight_constructs.foot_note: Population reliability (ω, α) and IRT parameters are estimated in calibration samples, not from a single session. Within-person spread and item-mean precision are descriptive statistics for this administration only. See docs/EIGHT_CONSTRUCT_MODEL.md.

cohortInsights.meta_description: Aggregate, probabilistic guidance for environments—group-level patterns only, for facilitation ideas.
cohortInsights.guidance_tagline: Insight → action · Guidance for human environments—reflection prompts, not a formal report.
cohortInsights.page_intro: This view turns pooled patterns into practical ideas for environments and facilitation—orientation for teams, not a scorecard or label.
cohortInsights.aggregate_only: No individual results appear here; only group-level shapes and suggestions.
cohortInsights.landscape_hint: Regions show where pooled emphasis clusters in a shared map (not individual locations).
cohortInsights.landscape_aria: Cohort cognitive landscape with regional highlights only
cohortInsights.env_optional_lead: These are optional ideas to consider—not requirements. Adapt what fits your context.
cohortInsights.dynamics_lead: Neutral read on differences that may show up together—focus on fit, not fault.
cohortInsights.confidence_tooltip: These insights are probabilistic and based on observed patterns, not definitive conclusions.
cohortInsights.pattern_library_body: When present, anonymized co-activation patterns add context. They describe tendencies in the aggregate, not categories for people.
cohortInsights.footer_uncertainty: Use these notes to prompt conversation and small experiments alongside your own professional judgment.

supportInsights.heading: Support insights
supportInsights.private_note: Private to this session — not shown in group views.
supportInsights.intro: Early hints based on your response field—suggestions you might explore alongside your own judgment.

banner.all_met: Research-grade confidence achieved across all dimensions.
banner.most_met: High confidence on most dimensions. Tap Continue assessment to strengthen the remaining ones.
banner.low_met: Assessment is indicative only. We recommend continuing the questionnaire for more reliable dimension scores.
banner.continue_hint: Returns you to the questionnaire to add more responses.

errorBoundary.results_body: We could not load or parse your saved session. Try completing the questionnaire again from the home page.
errorBoundary.questionnaire_body: The questionnaire hit an unexpected error. You can return home and try again.

questionnaire.session_mismatch: That link does not match this browser’s saved session. Open results from this device, or use Retake to start fresh.
questionnaire.refinement_badge: Refinement mode · Continuing to improve confidence on: {dims}
questionnaire.refinement_badge_generic: Refinement mode · Continuing to strengthen routing confidence.
questionnaire.page_subtitle: Answer honestly based on your typical experiences.
questionnaire.progress_coverage_hint: Coverage reflects how many prompts have touched each routing band (internal routing bookkeeping only).
questionnaire.progress_encourage_low: Keep going — we’re still gathering enough diverse responses for a stable summary.
questionnaire.progress_encourage_mid: Good progress — coverage is building across routing bands.
questionnaire.progress_encourage_high: Strong coverage — you can finish soon with a well-supported pipeline output.
questionnaire.help_bullet_1: Answer based on your typical behaviour.
questionnaire.help_bullet_3: Be honest for the most accurate profile.
questionnaire.help_bullet_4: You can withdraw at any time.
questionnaire.withdraw_confirm: Are you sure you want to withdraw? Your progress in this browser will be lost.
questionnaire.research_summary: Research information: how this question contributes to your profile
questionnaire.research_measures: This question measures the following cognitive dimensions:
questionnaire.question: Question
questionnaire.prompt_likert5: Please rate how much this statement describes you in your daily life.
questionnaire.prompt_likert3: Pick the option that fits you most. Short labels work well if this is read aloud.
questionnaire.likert5_strongly_disagree_desc: This does not describe me at all
questionnaire.likert3_low_desc: Rarely or not true for me
questionnaire.load_failed: Could not load questions. Check your connection or try again.

radar.caption: Radar chart comparing your raw scores (outer ring) with confidence-adjusted scores (inner ring). A wide gap on a spoke means we are less certain on that dimension.
radar.caption_high: A point near the outside means stronger endorsement on that dimension’s high end of the scale.
radar.caption_low: A point closer to the centre means responses leaned toward the low end of that dimension.
radar.aria: Radar chart of ten cognitive routing dimensions with raw and confidence-adjusted scores

bars.caption: Each bar shows your score between the low and high plain-language labels. The pale tail shows how high the score could plausibly reach given current uncertainty.
bars.aria: Dimensional bars: six primary routing scores first; T–V in an expandable section (ADR-007).
bars.research_routing_disclosure_summary: T, I, A, V — expand for the same bar layout (research-facing; ADR-007).
bars.tail_explainer: Striped segment: plausible upper range if confidence increased (not a statistical CI).
bars.progressbar_aria: {title}: {raw} percent raw score; plausible upper near {upper} percent

insights.caption: Short interpretations for each dimension. Cards with italic text are less certain — hover or focus the card for why.
insights.aria: Insight cards: six primary routing dimensions first; T–V in an expandable section (ADR-007).
insights.research_routing_disclosure_summary: T, I, A, V — same insight layout as the primary six; expand if you want these research-facing notes.
insights.confidence_note_generic: We are not yet fully confident on this dimension — consider answering more questions that touch this area.
insights.confidence_note_sample: We would need more high-loading answers to reach full research-grade confidence here.
insights.deemphasized_tooltip: Lower confidence: this card is shown muted until more consistent responses are collected.

F.title: Focus Intensity
F.description: How strongly you sustain attention on one stream of information versus shifting when distractions appear.

P.title: Pattern Processing
P.description: Preference for concrete procedures versus spotting rules, regularities, and abstract structure.

S.title: Sensory Sensitivity
S.description: How strongly light, sound, texture, and other sensory load affect comfort and concentration.

E.title: Social Energy
E.description: How social interaction affects your energy—drawing stimulation from others versus needing recovery time after groups.

R.title: Structure Preference
R.high_label: Strong preference for routine and plans
R.description: Comfort with fixed plans and predictable rhythms versus improvising when schedules or expectations shift.

C.title: Cognitive Flexibility
C.description: Ease of revising mental models when information is incomplete, contradictory, or changing.

T.title: Temporal Processing
T.description: Awareness of duration, pacing, and deadlines versus immersion where clock time fades into the background.

I.title: Interoceptive Awareness
I.description: How clearly internal bodily signals—tension, fatigue, hunger—reach conscious attention.

A.title: Associative Thinking
A.description: Tendency toward tight stepwise links versus wide, metaphor-rich, or tangential idea connections.

V.title: Verbal–Visual Processing
V.description: Relative weight of language and ordered steps versus imagery, layout, and spatial wholes when thinking.

F.high: You tend to sustain attention when something grips you, and distractions are relatively easy to set aside.
F.mid: Your focus shifts with context—sometimes deep, sometimes more easily pulled by what is around you.
F.low: You often notice competing demands on your attention and may switch tasks or stimuli more readily.

P.high: You may feel comfortable with abstract framing and often look for structure or rules beneath surface details.
P.mid: You mix concrete examples with occasional pattern-seeking, depending on the task at hand.
P.low: You prefer tangible steps and familiar procedures over open-ended abstraction.

S.high: Sensory inputs such as noise, light, or texture can strongly shape comfort and concentration.
S.mid: Some environments feel fine while others—especially busy or intense ones—wear on you faster.
S.low: Typical sensory load in everyday settings tends to bother you less than in busier profiles.

E.high: Social time tends to cost energy, and you recover best with quieter solo space afterwards.
E.mid: You enjoy some social contact but prefer it in measured doses rather than constant stimulation.
E.low: You draw energy from people and activities around you and tolerate longer social stretches well.

R.high: Predictable routines and clear plans usually help you feel settled and effective.
R.mid: You like some structure but can flex when plans need to change at short notice.
R.low: You adapt quickly when agendas shift and rarely depend on a fixed rhythm to feel okay.

C.high: You may be relatively comfortable revising views or switching mental gears when new information appears.
C.mid: You can adjust when needed, though rapid ambiguity or reversals may feel tiring at times.
C.low: You prefer stable answers and clear expectations over prolonged uncertainty.

T.high: You tend to notice pacing, intervals, and deadlines more sharply than losing track of time.
T.mid: Your sense of time shifts with context—sometimes precise, sometimes more present-focused.
T.low: Hours can compress or stretch unnoticed; you often live more in the present than on a clock.

I.high: Internal cues like hunger, fatigue, or tension tend to register clearly in awareness.
I.mid: Some body signals are clear while others arrive only after they are quite strong.
I.low: Physical states can sneak up on you before you consciously notice them.

A.high: Ideas often spark far-reaching links—metaphors, analogies, or tangents arrive readily.
A.mid: You blend straightforward paths with occasional leaps when a prompt invites creativity.
A.low: You usually keep to direct, stepwise links rather than wide associative hops.

V.high: Spatial layouts, diagrams, or mental imagery often carry weight over long verbal chains.
V.mid: You switch between words and images depending on what the task rewards.
V.low: Language and ordered steps tend to be your default handle on new information.

landscape.hero_title: Many thinking tendencies at once — patterns, not a single label
landscape.hero_body: Your answers shape several thinking tendencies together. Each one appears as its own dot. Dots that sit close together are somewhat alike in the model we use. This picture is a pattern map for reflection—plain-language tendencies, not a grade or rank.
landscape.map_caption: Each dot is one thinking tendency (size and clarity show how strong it is here). Faint lines link related tendencies; circles group nearby ones. The centre label is the balance point of your pattern—not a single “type”.
landscape.map_aria: Picture of several thinking tendencies at once, with simple reference examples
landscape.pattern_banner: Common combination (this device): {traits} · seen together after {support} completions here (~{pct}% of summaries stored in this browser) · overlap with you: {score}%
landscape.map_density_hint: The soft coloured wash shows where tendencies cluster in the picture; it is not a score chart or league table.
landscape.view_switcher_aria: Choose another way to look at the same picture
landscape.view_vector_activation_caption: Each row is one tendency in your constellation. The bar shows how strong it is in this session—not a school grade or rank.
landscape.view_vector_blend_caption: One vector derived from your activations for export and research tools. These coordinates are not the same as the coloured dimension bars.
landscape.view_vector_caption: Trait activations first; optional blended vector below for technical use.
landscape.view_vector_aria: List of trait activations and optional blended embedding coordinates
landscape.field_caption: A continuous cognitive field built from all activations. Peaks indicate multiple active regions, gradients show transitions between them.
landscape.view_terrain3d_caption: Same layout as Map and Density, shown as terrain: height follows the same smoothed trait density (taller = more combined emphasis there—not a separate score).
landscape.view_terrain3d_legend_peak: Elevation mirrors relative density. Peaks are where several tendencies stack in this projection—not a grade or ranking.
landscape.view_terrain3d_legend_you: The bright sphere is your balance point on the surface: the weighted centre of your activations in this 2D layout.
landscape.view_terrain3d_legend_rings: Coloured rings mark clusters—groups of trait dots that sit close together (same groupings as on the flat map).
landscape.view_terrain3d_legend_layout: Map, Density, and 3D share one coordinate system. UMAP or PCA only arranges the plane—left/right has no fixed meaning; compare shapes, not compass direction.
landscape.view_terrain3d_aria: Three-dimensional cognitive terrain: elevation shows relative density; you are the accent marker; rings show clusters
landscape.view_terrain3d_hint: Drag to orbit (3D) or rotate in plane (top-down). Scroll or pinch to zoom. Muted colours on purpose — this is exploratory, not a score.
landscape.constellation_note: The soft glow marks the balance point of all your tendency dots together—not a separate score or person type.
landscape.map_zoom_hint: Scroll or pinch to zoom; use the buttons to reset.
landscape.map_proxy_note: This link did not include a full saved summary; the picture uses a careful estimate from the shared scores only.
landscape.share_url_too_long: This link would exceed a safe URL length (2000 characters). Export JSON or use a shorter page URL.
landscape.share_copied: Shareable link copied to the clipboard.
landscape.export_png_error: Could not export PNG (browser blocked image export). Try a screenshot instead.
landscape.bars_caption: Each bar uses colour for the dimension; opacity reflects routing confidence. Hover or long-press a row for the short scientific description.
landscape.research_routing_disclosure_summary: T, I, A, V — extended routing dimensions (lower item coverage; research-facing per ADR-007). Expand to see the same bars.

url_profile.summary: This view was opened from a shared link. It shows dimension scores only — not a full saved session.

offline.pending_sync_banner: {n} completed session(s) waiting to upload when online (optional cloud sync).
offline.pending_sync_export_aria: Save one full-session JSON file per queued offline session

field.offline_tools_body: If you have no network: import the same question bank JSON you ship with this build, or load the bundled copy, then continue the assessment.
field.import_page_lead: Paste CSV with columns questionId, response (1–5 or 1–3), optional responseTimeMs and timestamp (ISO). The same scoring pipeline runs as in the app.
field.import_disclaimer: Use only with consent and ethical approval. Verify questionIds match your printed form.
field.bank_error_hint: If you are offline with no prior visit: import the question bank JSON, use “Load bundled bank”, or open /data/question-bank-universal-all.json from this deployment.

group_analysis.title: Group cognitive overview
group_analysis.meta_description: Optional multi-profile overview: diversity, environment signals, and facilitation hints from combined share payloads.
group_analysis.lead: Combine several shareable summaries (the same encoding as your results link) to explore diversity, environment fit, and facilitation ideas. This is an add-on: it does not replace your personal map or the separate cohort insights demo.
group_analysis.instructions: Paste one payload per person. You can paste the full share URL, or only the `p=` parameter, or the raw base64 block from the link.
group_analysis.error_parse: Could not parse one or more entries. Check each field.
group_analysis.error_min: Add at least two valid profiles.
group_analysis.disclaimer: Aggregate, descriptive guidance for facilitation—patterns for conversation, not grades or hiring signals. Use only with appropriate consent when combining profiles.
group_analysis.setting_context_hint: Rates the environment, not people. Used only for aggregate person–environment mismatch hints.

pattern.answer_consistency_high: High answer consistency across similar prompts

note.response_time_variance: Response times vary substantially across items.
note.entropy_high: Response sequence shows higher exploration across scale positions.

summary.confident: The session supports a moderately confident feature summary.
summary.limited: Feature confidence is limited; interpret with caution.
summary.preliminary: Limited response signal; treat any patterns as preliminary.

pipeline.patterns_fallback: No strong distinctive patterns stood out from this session’s statistics alone.
pipeline.notes_fallback: This is a descriptive summary for reflection and research—plain-language tendencies from your answers.

welcome.title: How does your mind work?
welcome.subtitle: A research tool that explores cognitive style as a continuous profile—plain-language patterns for reflection and research.

consent.title: Research Consent Form
consent.about_body: We're studying how people think and process information in different ways. This research helps us understand that everyone's mind works uniquely, like how some people love detailed plans while others prefer to be spontaneous. Your participation helps us create better tools for self-understanding.
consent.do_1: Answer questions about how you think and prefer to do things (10-15 minutes)
consent.do_2: Get a personal profile showing your unique thinking style
consent.do_3: Receive helpful insights about your cognitive strengths
consent.privacy_anonymous_body: We don't ask for your name, email, or any personal information
consent.privacy_stop_body: You can quit at any moment, no questions asked
consent.privacy_secure_body: Your answers are protected and stored safely
consent.privacy_research_body: Information used only to understand thinking patterns
consent.important_medical: This tool is for learning how you tend to think and for contributing to research. It describes tendencies on continuous scales—it does not assess health or fitness for any purpose.
consent.important_help: If you need help with mental health, please talk to a doctor, counselor, or trusted healthcare professional.
consent.helps_body: Your anonymous answers, combined with many others, help scientists understand how different people think. This research may be shared in scientific papers to help educators, researchers, and people everywhere better understand cognitive diversity.
consent.age_body: You must be 18 years or older to participate. If you're under 18, please ask a parent or guardian to review this with you.
consent.checkbox_body: I am 18 years or older. I have read and understand all the information above. I freely choose to participate in this research. I know I can stop at any time. I understand my answers will be kept anonymous and used only for research.
consent.footer_ethics: This research follows ethical guidelines for human subjects research.

post_consent.hero_subtitle: Explore how you tend to think and learn—descriptive profiles on continuous dimensions
post_consent.card_evidence_body: Grounded in cognitive science and dimensional summaries for descriptive, self-report use
post_consent.card_adaptive_body: Questions adapt based on your responses for personalized insights
post_consent.card_insights_body: See plain-language ideas that may fit your style—suggestions only, not prescriptions
post_consent.diff_traditional_1: Sorting people into fixed categorical boxes
post_consent.start_meta: Approximately 10-15 minutes • Completely anonymous • Research contribution

sms_profile.title: Profile from SMS code
sms_profile.intro: Paste a compact code (e.g. F7P8S3…) produced on the results page. Deciles are approximate—coarse routing summaries for relay only.
sms_profile.invalid: Could not read this code. Check all ten dimensions are present.
sms_profile.twilio_note: Sending SMS requires a Twilio (or similar) backend — not bundled in this repository.

introduction.footer_ethics: This research follows ethical guidelines for human subjects research.
introduction.footer_contact: If you have questions, please contact: contact@synapticfour.com
introduction.step1_p1: This is a research tool designed to help understand how different people think and process information.
introduction.step1_p2: The assessment explores ten dimensions of cognitive style using evidence-informed questions.
introduction.step1_p3: Your participation contributes to scientific understanding of cognitive diversity.
introduction.step2_p1: The assessment consists of questions about your preferences and typical behaviours.
introduction.step2_p2: Most questions use a 5-point scale from “Strongly disagree” to “Strongly agree”.
introduction.step2_p3: The questionnaire adapts based on your responses to provide the most relevant questions.
introduction.step3_p1: This assessment is based on established cognitive science research.
introduction.step3_p2: It measures continuous dimensions rather than categorical labels.
introduction.step3_p3: Results include confidence levels to indicate reliability of measurements.
introduction.step4_p1: Your participation is completely anonymous. No personal identifying information is collected.
introduction.step4_p2: You can stop at any time without penalty.
introduction.step4_p3: Your data will be used only for research purposes and may be shared in aggregated form. Optional anonymous sync to a research database applies only when this deployment has cloud storage configured; otherwise your session stays on this device unless you export or share it yourself.
introduction.step5_p1: This tool describes how you tend to think—for reflection and research questions about cognitive style.
introduction.step5_p2: Results represent tendencies, not definitive characteristics.
introduction.step5_p3: Individual variation is normal and expected.
introduction.step5_p4: If you have health concerns, please consult a qualified professional.

landing.lead: About 10–15 minutes · No registration · Please read the short consent before you start.
landing.diff_heading: How this differs from label-based screeners
landing.diff_1: Maps tendencies on a continuous spectrum—patterns, not categories.
landing.diff_2: Plain-language profile: no labels, no scores, no pass/fail.
landing.diff_3: For self-understanding and research—not for institutions to rank or screen people.
landing.get_1: 🗺️ A multi-dimensional map of your cognitive style

results_interpretation.heading: What does this mean?
results_interpretation.para1: Your profile shows how you weight focus, pattern processing, sensory sensitivity, social energy, structure preference, and cognitive flexibility. Everyone combines these dimensions in a unique way.
results_interpretation.para2: In research literature, similar shapes often appear among broad themes of cognitive diversity and everyday processing styles—your snapshot here is only a dimensional description and is not interchangeable with any single research theme. For more on the research framing, read the <research>PCMS research note on Synaptic Four</research>.
results_interpretation.strengths_sub: Your three highest scores among the core six dimensions — descriptive strengths for reflection.

ethics_consent.page_intro_cal: You're about to explore your cognitive profile. Please take a moment to read what we collect and why.
ethics_consent.prototype_banner_body: PCMS is a voluntary research questionnaire in active development. It maps self-reported tendencies on continuous dimensions for scientific learning about cognitive diversity. Scores are not yet validated for placement, screening, or eligibility decisions.
ethics_consent.irb_status_body: This tool has not yet received institutional research ethics approval as a finished study. Data you allow us to receive may be used for instrument development and aggregate research reporting only.
ethics_consent.confirm_label: I have read this section carefully and confirm my understanding.
ethics_consent.qa_mode_banner: QA build: full consent ladder (every section on its own screen). Set NEXT_PUBLIC_PCMS_CONSENT_MODE=default for the shorter flow.
ethics_consent.storage_notice: This application stores session data locally in your browser and, if you consent, anonymously on a research server. No directly identifying personal data are collected in the assessment flow.
ethics_consent.storage_notice_local: Session data are stored locally in this browser. The assessment flow does not collect your name, email, or postal address.
ethics_consent.storage_notice_cloud_extra: This deployment has optional cloud storage: if you continue, anonymous session summaries may sync to the configured research database (see Privacy). If cloud storage is not in use, nothing leaves this device except what you export or share.
ethics_consent.research_body: PCMS is a voluntary research questionnaire. It explores self-reported tendencies on continuous dimensions to support scientific understanding of cognitive diversity. Use it for reflection and research participation; treatment planning and formal eligibility decisions belong with qualified professionals.
ethics_consent.measured_intro: The assessment asks about preferences and typical behaviours. Ten dimensions summarise patterns (for example focus, sensory comfort, or planning style). Each score is a tendency on a scale—descriptive, not a ranking of overall ability.
ethics_consent.limits_body: The questionnaire records self-reported tendencies on the dimensions described above. It focuses on cognitive style patterns—IQ, global ability ratings, and pass/fail outcomes are out of scope. If you have health concerns, contact a qualified professional.
ethics_consent.privacy_body: Synaptic Four (Stuttgart) stores an anonymous session identifier, timestamps, your answers, response times, and derived statistical summaries needed for research. We do not collect your name, email, or postal address in this flow unless you contact us separately (which is a separate processing context). Full privacy notice: “Privacy” in the footer or /privacy.
ethics_consent.privacy_body_local: Synaptic Four (Stuttgart) uses an anonymous session identifier, timestamps, your answers, response times, and derived statistical summaries for this tool. By default these stay in this browser for your session; retention and deletion options are described on the Privacy page. We do not collect your name, email, or postal address in this flow unless you contact us separately (then it is a separate processing context).
ethics_consent.privacy_body_cloud_extra: When optional cloud storage is enabled for this deployment, the same pseudonymous fields may be written to the configured research database if sync succeeds. Full privacy notice: “Privacy” in the footer or /privacy.
ethics_consent.rights_body: You may stop at any time without penalty. You may request deletion of data linked to your session ID where technically possible (use “Delete my data” on the results page when enabled). Contact: contact@synapticfour.com for requests and GDPR rights; details are on the Privacy page.
ethics_consent.ghana_body: Voluntary self-report for research and reflection through Synaptic Four’s open mapping tool. For medical questions or care, speak with a licensed doctor, nurse, psychologist, community health worker, or trusted local practitioner. Your participation supports instrument development and stays in the research-and-reflection lane with the project team.

ethics_assent.title: Before you view your results
ethics_assent.intro: Please confirm how you will use these results and acknowledge their limits.
ethics_assent.chk_nondiagnostic: I understand these results describe tendencies for reflection and research questions about cognitive style.
ethics_assent.chk_research: I understand this tool supports self-reflection and research participation; treatment or placement decisions stay with qualified professionals.
ethics_assent.error_incomplete: Please answer all fields and tick both confirmations.

ethics_results.non_medical: Your profile summarizes self-reported tendencies on the questionnaire dimensions.
ethics_results.non_diagnosis: Best used for reflection and research questions about environment fit—decisions about roles, school, or care need broader evidence.
ethics_results.non_judgement: Your profile is neither good nor bad — it describes tendencies, not worth.
ethics_results.ghana_medical: For medical questions or care, speak with a licensed doctor, nurse, counsellor, or traditional healer you trust—this mapping stays in the research-and-reflection lane.
ethics_results.delete_help: Removes server-side rows for your session ID (where configured) and clears this browser.

ethics_delete.confirm_body: This will request deletion of remote data for your session (if the server is configured) and clear local results in this browser.
ethics_delete.done: Deletion request completed. Local data cleared.
ethics_delete.error: Deletion could not be completed. You can still clear this browser’s storage from site settings.

operatorSync.env_on: Cloud env: variables set (writes can still fail at runtime).
operatorSync.env_off: Cloud env: not set — no research database upload in this build.
operatorSync.last_never: Last remote write: none logged in this browser yet.

facilitator.section_intro: Practical ideas for teachers and parents, grounded in this session’s dimension scores (suggestions only).
facilitator.disclaimer: These observations are based on self-reported tendencies and are not validated for individual educational decisions. Always combine with your direct knowledge of the child.
facilitator.low_confidence_note: Overall session confidence was limited — treat these cards as tentative prompts, not a full picture of the learner.

filler1.observation: Several dimensions sit in a middle range here, so day-to-day needs may shift with the task, sleep, and stress.
filler1.suggestion: Offering a predictable routine with small choices built in may help many learners in this range feel steady without feeling boxed in.
filler1.professional: If learning or wellbeing concerns persist across settings, a trusted educator or health professional can help you think through next steps together.

filler2.observation: Self-report tools capture a moment in time; behaviour in class can look different when interests, peers, or sensory load change.
filler2.suggestion: Short check-ins after varied activities (quiet work, group talk, movement) may help you notice what supports engagement most often.
filler2.professional: If you are unsure how to interpret patterns over time, a specialist who knows the child can offer perspective without labelling them.

high.observation: May stay absorbed in a task for a long stretch and lose track of transitions or class-wide announcements.
high.suggestion: Gentle transition signals a minute ahead may help. A quiet corner for extended work may help when the room is lively.
high.professional: If long stretches of solo focus get in the way of required group work or safety routines, a specialist conversation may help find balanced supports.

low.observation: May notice side conversations, movement, or small visual changes in the room while working.
low.suggestion: Seating with a simple visual barrier to the busiest part of the room may help. Breaking tasks into short visible steps may help more than long open-ended blocks.
low.professional: If attention challenges clearly affect learning despite adjustments, an educational or developmental specialist may help explore what else could help.

high.observation: May enjoy open-ended problems and spotting patterns before step-by-step instructions feel necessary.
high.suggestion: Optional extension prompts or choice boards may help. Very rigid step lists without the “why” may feel constraining.
high.professional: If frustration appears mainly when tasks stay purely concrete, a learning specialist may help match task design to strengths.

low.observation: May follow clear examples best and feel more secure when instructions are concrete and ordered.
low.suggestion: Worked examples, checklists, and labelled materials may help. Open-ended prompts without models may feel harder to start.
low.professional: If anxiety or refusal shows up mostly on vague tasks, a professional may help design scaffolds that still build independence over time.

high.observation: May be easily distracted by background noise or bright lighting.
high.suggestion: Seating away from windows or high-traffic areas may help. Softer lighting or optional quiet headphones during independent work may help.
high.professional: If sensory sensitivity clearly interferes with learning or wellbeing, a specialist assessment may help identify specific supports.

low.observation: May tolerate busy or varied sensory environments more comfortably than peers who need quieter conditions.
low.suggestion: Front-row or central seating may still work well; just watch for fatigue on very long intense days.
low.professional: If you ever worry about hearing or vision rather than comfort alone, a routine screening may help rule out basic sensory needs.

high.observation: May need recovery time after lively group work or whole-class discussions.
high.suggestion: Pair or trio tasks before large groups may help. A brief quiet reset after noisy activities may help energy return.
high.professional: If social exhaustion leads to frequent distress or withdrawal, a counsellor or trusted clinician may help explore coping strategies.

low.observation: May perk up with discussion, collaboration, or movement between peers.
low.suggestion: Rotating roles in group tasks and verbal rehearsal may help. Long silent independent blocks may feel draining.
low.professional: If restlessness is paired with marked distress at home or school, a professional may help separate social energy from other stressors.

high.observation: May feel safer when schedules, steps, and expectations are spelled out in advance.
high.suggestion: Visible timetables and advance warning of changes may help. Surprise rule shifts may feel harder to absorb in the moment.
high.professional: If rigidity around routines causes intense upset, a specialist may help introduce flexibility skills gently.

low.observation: May adapt on the fly when plans change and tolerate loose timelines better than highly fixed routines.
low.suggestion: Framing goals as outcomes rather than minute-by-minute scripts may help. Too many arbitrary rules may feel unnecessary.
low.professional: If improvisation leads to missed deadlines across subjects, a learning coach may help build light structure without removing autonomy.

high.observation: May hold multiple possible answers comfortably and revise ideas when new information appears.
high.suggestion: Tasks that allow revising drafts or comparing strategies may help. Very black-and-white grading without room to explain may feel tight.
high.professional: If uncertainty feels exciting at school but overwhelming at home (or the reverse), a brief conversation with a counsellor may help.

low.observation: May prefer clear right-or-wrong framing and notice discomfort when instructions or answers feel ambiguous.
low.suggestion: Rubrics with examples, worked answers, and explicit “good enough” criteria may help. Open-ended problems may need a starting template.
low.professional: If distress centres on small changes to familiar routines or wording, a professional may help build tolerance step by step.

selfNomination.heading: Do any of these resonate?
selfNomination.subheading: These are descriptions of experiences some people have. Select any that feel true for you — or none. There's no right answer.
selfNomination.disclaimer: Your selections are for your own reflection and optional research. They don't affect your map.
selfNomination.research_skipped: Participant skipped the self-nomination step — no descriptor selections stored.
selfNomination.research_explicit_none: Participant chose “None of these” — zero descriptors selected (explicit).
selfNomination.research_disclaimer: Routing scores shown here are the same PCMS session outputs as elsewhere; they are not recomputed from descriptor picks. Descriptive only — selections are not scored (ADR-003).

docsPages.ethics_meta_description: Ethics framework and appropriate use of the Perceptual & Cognitive Mapping System research prototype.
docsPages.ethics_intro: Full project ethics document (same content as the open repository). For questions, use the contact in the footer.
docsPages.validation_meta_description: What evidence exists today for PCMS scores and what the system cannot claim.
docsPages.validation_intro: Plain-language validation status for reviewers and participants (same document as the open repository).

site_footer.prototype_line: Research prototype for reflection and open science — maps cognitive tendencies; open MIT-licensed project.
